Honor is inappropriate for those who are unworthy and unfit for it.
Whoever is cursed without cause, the curse will do him no harm any more than a bird flying over his head.

Every creature must be dealt with according to its nature, but careless and profligate sinners never will be ruled by reason and persuasion. People are indeed born like a wild ass's colt; but some, by the grace of God, are changed.
We should fit our remarks to the person, and address them to his conscience, so as may best end the debate.
Fools are not fit to be trusted, nor to have any honor. Wise sayings, as a foolish man delivers and applies them, lose their usefulness.
This verse may either declare how the Lord, the Creator of all men, will deal with sinners according to their guilt, or how the powerful among people should disgrace and punish the wicked.
The dog is a loathsome emblem of those sinners who return to their vices.
We see many a one who has some little sense, but is proud of it. This describes those who think their spiritual state to be good, when really it is very bad.

The slothful man hates everything that requires care and labor. But it is foolish to frighten ourselves from real duties by fancied difficulties. This may be applied to a person slothful in the duties of religion.

Having seen the slothful man in fear of his work, here we find him in love with his ease. Bodily ease is the sad occasion of many spiritual diseases. He does not care to get forward with his business. Slothful professors turn thus. The world and the flesh are hinges on which they are hung; and though they move in a course of outward services, yet they are not the nearer to heaven.

The sluggard is now out of his bed, but he might have lain there, for anything he is likely to bring to pass in his work. It is common for people who will not do their duty, to pretend they cannot. Those that are slothful in religion, will not be at the pains to feed their souls with the bread of life, nor to fetch in promised blessings by prayer.
He who takes pains in religion, knows he is working for a good Master, and that his labor will not be in vain.
To make ourselves busy in other people's matters, is to thrust ourselves into temptation.
He who sins in jest, must repent in earnest, or his sin will be his ruin.
Contention heats the spirit, and puts families and societies into a flame. And that fire is commonly kindled and kept burning by whisperers and backbiters.
A wicked heart disguising itself, is like a potsherd covered with the dross of silver.
Always distrust when a person speaks fair unless you know him well. Satan, in his temptations, speaks fair, as he did to Eve; but it is madness to give credit to him.
What pains people take to do mischief to others! But it is digging a pit, it is rolling a stone, hard work; and they prepare mischief to themselves.

There are two sorts of lies equally detestable. A slandering lie, the mischief of this everyone sees. A flattering lie, which secretly works ruin. A wise person will be more afraid of a flatterer than of a slanderer.
